Ridge regression with winsorized growth (2.5/97.5 pctile), growth^2 for non-linearity, and log|growth| for diminishing returns. Target multiples also winsorized. 3 features, all growth-derived with economic rationale: linear growth premium, convex high-growth premium, and diminishing marginal valuation benefit.

Ridge regression (lambda=5) on log-transformed valuation multiples. Features: winsorized growth% (linear growth premium), log|growth| (diminishing returns), gross margin (profitability premium), growth×margin interaction (quality-growth synergy), and sector dummies (SaaS, Software, Semi, Hardware, Health, Payments, Cybersecurity) capturing systematic industry valuation differences. 11 features with strong economic rationale. Log target reduces outlier influence; predictions exponentiated back.

Ridge regression with Huber-robust reweighting on winsorized data (5/95 pctile). 5 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium, (3) log|growth| — diminishing returns, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev, (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy. Features standardized before fitting for numerical stability. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=2) with standardized features on winsorized data (5/95 pctile). 3 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity in market's willingness to pay for faster growth, (3) log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit at extreme growth rates. Target multiples winsorized to reduce outlier influence. Features standardized before fitting for numerical stability and comparable regularization across features.

Ridge regression (lambda=1) with standardized features on winsorized data (2.5/97.5 pctile). 8 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium, (3) log|growth| — diminishing returns at extreme growth, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev, (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy, (6) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic premium, (7) Software dummy — software sector valuation effect, (8) Semiconductor dummy — hardware sector valuation discount. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=1) with standardized features on winsorized data. 5 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity, (3) log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev multiples (higher margin companies convert more revenue to profit, justifying higher multiples), (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy where high-growth, high-margin companies deserve disproportionate premium. Target winsorized at 1/99 pctile to reduce extreme outlier influence. Features standardized before fitting for proper regularization across scales.

Huber-robust Ridge regression (lambda=1, 3 IRLS iterations) with standardized features on winsorized data. 5 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity, (3) log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev multiples (higher margin => more revenue converts to profit, justifying higher multiples), (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy where high-growth, high-margin companies deserve disproportionate premium. Gross margin filtered to [0, 1.5] range for validity. Target winsorized at 2/98 pctile; Huber reweighting downweights residual outliers using MAD-based scale estimation. Features standardized before fitting.

Ridge regression (lambda=2) with standardized features on aggressively winsorized data. 6 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth²/100 — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit at extreme growth, (4) positive growth indicator — discrete valuation jump for growing vs declining companies, (5) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium (higher margin => more revenue converts to profit, justifying higher EV/Rev multiples), (6) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy where high-growth, high-margin companies deserve disproportionate premium. Target winsorized at 2/98 pctile; growth at 5/95 pctile. Features standardized before fitting. All features have clear economic rationale.

Ridge regression (lambda=2) with standardized features on winsorized data (2/98 target, 2.5/97.5 growth). 8 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev multiples (higher margin companies convert more revenue to profit, justifying higher multiples), (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy where high-growth, high-margin companies deserve disproportionate premium, (6) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic premium due to recurring revenue, (7) Software dummy — software sector valuation effect from high scalability, (8) Semiconductor dummy — hardware sector with different capital intensity profile. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=2) with trimmed observations and standardized features. 8 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity in willingness to pay, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit at extreme growth rates, (4) positive growth indicator — discrete valuation jump for growing vs declining companies (market pays a premium simply for positive growth trajectory), (5) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic premium due to recurring revenue and high switching costs, (6) Software dummy — software sector valuation effect from high scalability and margins, (7) Semiconductor dummy — hardware sector with different capital intensity and cyclicality, (8) Cybersecurity dummy — security companies command premium due to secular demand tailwinds. Observations trimmed at 2/98 pctile on target and 2.5/97.5 on growth to remove distorted data points rather than winsorizing them. Features standardized before fitting for proper regularization across scales. All features have clear economic rationale.

Ridge regression (lambda=1) with standardized features on winsorized data. 5 features: (1) winsorized growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear high-growth premium capturing convexity, (3) log|growth| — diminishing marginal valuation benefit, (4) gross margin (GP/Rev) — profitability premium driving EV/Rev multiples (higher margin companies convert more revenue to profit, justifying higher multiples), (5) growth × margin interaction — quality-growth synergy where high-growth, high-margin companies deserve disproportionate premium. Target winsorized at 1/99 pctile to reduce extreme outlier influence. Features standardized before fitting for proper regularization across scales.

Ridge regression (lambda=5) with metric-type-aware features and standardization. For P/EPS: 5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) growth² — non-linear premium capturing convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal PE expansion at extreme growth rates, (4) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (5) sqrt|growth| — concave growth-PE relationship reflecting diminishing returns. For EV/Rev: uses gross margin and growth×margin interaction instead. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 200) range to exclude distorted ratios. Target winsorized at 2/98 pctile; growth at 2.5/97.5. Higher Ridge lambda (5) for temporal stability across market regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Huber-robust regression (epsilon=1.5, alpha=5) with metric-type-aware features and standardization. For P/EPS: 7 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) growth² — non-linear premium capturing convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal PE expansion at extreme growth rates, (4) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (5) sqrt|growth| — concave growth-PE relationship reflecting diminishing returns, (6) log(forward EPS) — earnings level as quality/maturity proxy (higher absolute EPS indicates more stable business with different PE characteristics), (7) growth × log(FE) interaction — growth premium modulated by earnings quality (growth is more valuable for companies with higher base earnings). For EV/Rev: 5 features using gross margin and growth×margin interaction instead. Huber loss naturally downweights PE outliers without aggressive winsorization. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 150) range; forward EPS filtered to >$0.10. Target winsorized at 2/98 pctile; growth at 2.5/97.5. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=5) with log-transformed PE target and metric-type-aware features. For P/EPS: log(PE) is regressed on 5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium, (2) growth² — non-linear convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms, (3) signed log|growth| — diminishing marginal PE expansion at extreme growth, (4) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for positive earnings trajectory, (5) |growth| — growth magnitude capturing symmetric valuation sensitivity. Log-transforming PE ratios is economically motivated: PE is right-skewed and the growth-PE relationship is multiplicative (growth drives percentage PE changes, not absolute). Predictions are exponentiated back to PE space. For EV/Rev: 5 features using gross margin and growth×margin interaction on original scale. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 200); target winsorized at 2/98 pctile before log. Growth winsorized at 2.5/97.5. Features standardized before fitting. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=10) with simplified, robust features and standardization. For P/EPS: 3 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty. For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE (tighter to handle fat tails); growth at 3/97. Higher Ridge lambda (10) for maximum temporal stability across market regimes. Fewer features reduce multicollinearity from correlated nonlinear transforms. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=5) with sector dummies and growth transforms. For P/EPS: 8 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings, (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect on valuation uncertainty, (4) growth²/100 — convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms (market pays exponentially more for faster growers), (5) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic PE premium due to recurring revenue and high switching costs, (6) Software dummy — software sector PE premium from high scalability and margins, (7) Semiconductor dummy — semi sector with different cyclicality and capital intensity affecting PE, (8) Tech dummy — broad technology sector effect capturing secular growth premium. For EV/Rev: 6 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and 3 sector dummies. For EV/GP: 6 features using growth, positive indicator, log growth, and 3 sector dummies. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Sector dummies capture persistent cross-sectional valuation differences that growth alone misses. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=15) with targeted sector dummies and forward EPS for stability. For P/EPS: 6 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) log(forward EPS) — earnings level as quality/maturity proxy (higher absolute EPS indicates more stable, mature business that typically trades at lower PE), (5) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic PE premium due to recurring revenue and high switching costs, (6) Software dummy — software sector PE premium from high scalability and margins. For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and SaaS dummy. For EV/GP: 4 features using growth, positive indicator, log growth, and SaaS dummy. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Higher Ridge lambda (15) for maximum temporal stability across market regimes. Sector dummies limited to SaaS and Software (most persistent valuation premiums) to avoid overfitting from too many dummies. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=20) with forward EPS level and robust growth features. For P/EPS: 5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) growth²/100 — convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms (market pays exponentially more for faster growers, capturing the nonlinear growth-PE relationship), (5) log(forward EPS) — earnings level as quality/maturity proxy (higher absolute EPS indicates more stable, mature business that typically trades at lower PE; this is the well-known inverse relationship between earnings yield and PE ratio). For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (3, 120) range for tighter outlier control. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Higher Ridge lambda (20) for maximum temporal stability across market regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression with adaptive lambda and growth convexity feature. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) growth²/100 — convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms (market pays exponentially more for faster growers, well-documented in valuation literature). For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 120) range for tighter outlier control. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Adaptive Ridge lambda scales with p/n ratio for optimal bias-variance tradeoff across different cross-section sizes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=12) with forward EPS level and robust features. For P/EPS: 5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) 1/forward EPS — captures the well-known inverse relationship between earnings level and PE ratio (low-EPS firms are early-stage with more option value, systematically trading at higher PEs independent of growth), (5) growth × 1/FE interaction — growth premium amplified for low-EPS firms reflecting operating leverage (growth is more valuable when base earnings are small). For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (3, 120) range; target winsorized at 5/95 pctile (aggressive tail trimming for PE stability); growth at 3/97. Ridge lambda=12 for temporal stability across market regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Huber robust regression (epsilon=1.35, alpha=0.01) with standardized features. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) log(forward EPS) — captures the well-known inverse relationship between earnings level and PE ratio (low-EPS firms are early-stage with option value, trading at systematically higher PEs). For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and signed log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 120) range for tighter outlier control. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Huber loss (epsilon=1.35) naturally downweights outlier observations without removing them, providing more stable coefficient estimates across time periods than OLS or Ridge. Light L2 regularization (alpha=0.01) since Huber already handles outlier influence. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=8) with simplified, robust features and standardization. For P/EPS: 3 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty. For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction. For EV/GP: 3 features using growth, positive indicator, and signed log growth. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Ridge lambda=8 tuned for optimal bias-variance tradeoff — lower than lambda=10 to allow more signal through while still providing temporal stability. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=8) on LOG-TRANSFORMED target with rank-based features. Key innovation: log(multiple) as target reduces heteroscedasticity in PE/EV ratios (right-skewed distributions become symmetric in log space), improving OLS efficiency and OOS stability. Predictions exponentiated back to level space. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) growth percentile rank [0,1] — robust monotonic growth premium immune to outliers and regime-dependent scaling, (2) growth rank squared — captures convexity where very high growth firms command disproportionate PE premiums, (3) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for positive earnings trajectory, (4) scaled growth %/100 — preserves cardinal magnitude information lost in ranking. For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth rank, rank², gross margin, and rank×margin interaction (growth more valuable for high-margin businesses). For EV/GP: 4 features using growth rank, rank², positive indicator, and scaled growth. Target log-winsorized at 2/98 pctile; growth winsorized at 2/98 before ranking. Rank features provide temporal stability since cross-sectional ordering is more persistent than absolute growth levels across bull/bear regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Huber robust regression (epsilon=1.5, alpha=1.0) with sector dummies and growth transforms. For P/EPS: 8 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) growth²/100 — convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms (market pays exponentially more for faster growers), (4) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect on valuation uncertainty, (5) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic PE premium due to recurring revenue and high switching costs, (6) Software dummy — software sector PE premium from high scalability and margins, (7) Semiconductor dummy — semi sector with different cyclicality and capital intensity affecting PE, (8) Tech dummy — broad technology sector effect capturing secular growth premium. For EV/Rev: 6 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, growth², and 2 sector dummies. For EV/GP: 6 features using growth, positive indicator, signed log growth, growth², and 2 sector dummies. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Huber loss (epsilon=1.5) naturally downweights outlier observations without removing them, providing more stable coefficient estimates across time periods than OLS or Ridge. Moderate L2 regularization (alpha=1.0) for temporal stability. Sector dummies capture persistent cross-sectional valuation differences that growth alone misses. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Quantile (median) regression via IRLS with Ridge regularization (alpha=8) for robust valuation prediction. Median regression minimizes absolute deviations rather than squared errors, making coefficient estimates naturally robust to the heavy-tailed PE distribution without aggressive winsorization. For P/EPS: 5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) log(forward EPS) — captures the well-known inverse relationship between earnings level and PE ratio (low-EPS firms are early-stage with more option value, systematically trading at higher PEs independent of growth rate), (5) growth²/100 — convexity in PE expansion for high-growth firms. For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and growth² convexity. For EV/GP: 4 features using growth, positive indicator, signed log growth, and growth². PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. IRLS quantile regression initialized from Ridge solution for fast convergence. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Log-space Ridge regression (lambda=10) with Duan's smearing back-transform. Key innovation: log-transforming the target (PE, EV/Rev, EV/GP) reduces heteroscedasticity and right-skewness, making the growth-valuation relationship more linear and stable across market regimes. Predictions back-transformed via exp() with Duan's smearing estimator for unbiased level predictions. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing firms, (3) growth²/100 — convexity capturing exponential PE premium for high-growth, (4) log(forward EPS) — earnings level effect (higher EPS = lower PE, well-known inverse relationship reflecting maturity and stability). For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and growth² convexity. For EV/GP: 4 features using growth, positive indicator, signed log growth, and growth² convexity. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 200) range. Log target winsorized at 2/98 pctile; growth at 3/97. Ridge lambda=10 for temporal stability. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=5) with piecewise-linear growth and aggressive filtering. For P/EPS: 2 features: (1) positive growth part (max(growth,0)) — captures the PE expansion slope for growing companies, (2) negative growth part (min(growth,0)) — captures the PE contraction slope for declining companies. Piecewise-linear allows asymmetric slopes: the market rewards growth more steeply than it punishes contraction (well-documented asymmetry in PE-growth relationships). PE observations aggressively pre-filtered to (3, 100) range to exclude distressed and speculative names that add noise. Target winsorized at 5/95 pctile for very tight tail control. For EV/Rev: 3 features using growth, gross margin, and growth² convexity. For EV/GP: 2 features using growth and positive growth indicator. Growth winsorized at 3/97. Ridge lambda=5 (lower since cleaner data needs less regularization). Fewer features (2-3) minimize multicollinearity and maximize adjusted R². All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=10) with growth convexity and forward EPS level. For P/EPS: 4-5 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect where both large positive and negative growth increase valuation uncertainty, (4) growth²/100 — convexity capturing exponential PE premium for high-growth firms, (5) log(forward EPS) when available — captures the well-known inverse relationship between earnings level and PE ratio (low-EPS firms are early-stage with more option value, systematically trading at higher PEs independent of growth rate). For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and growth² convexity. For EV/GP: 4 features using growth, positive indicator, signed log growth, and growth² convexity. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Ridge lambda=10 for temporal stability across market regimes. Growth² captures the well-documented convexity in growth-valuation relationships — the marginal PE premium per unit of growth increases at higher growth rates. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=8) with industry dummies and growth features for cross-sectional valuation prediction. Key innovation: adding persistent industry-level intercept shifts (SaaS, Software, Semiconductor, S&P 500 membership) to capture structural valuation differences that growth rates alone cannot explain. For P/EPS: 7 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) growth²/100 — convexity capturing exponential PE premium for high-growth firms (well-documented non-linearity in growth-PE relationship), (4) SaaS dummy — SaaS companies trade at systematic PE premium due to recurring revenue, high switching costs, and predictable cash flows, (5) Software dummy — software sector PE premium from high scalability, low marginal costs, and high operating leverage, (6) Semiconductor dummy — semi sector with different cyclicality and capital intensity affecting PE (typically lower PE due to cyclical earnings), (7) S&P 500 membership — large-cap quality premium (index inclusion signals institutional quality, liquidity premium, and passive fund demand). For EV/Rev: 6 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and 3 industry dummies (SaaS, Software, Semi). For EV/GP: 6 features using growth, positive indicator, growth², and 3 industry dummies. PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. Ridge lambda=8 for temporal stability across market regimes while allowing industry effects to express. Industry dummies are standardized like continuous features so Ridge regularization applies proportionally. All features have clear economic rationale.

Huber robust regression (epsilon=1.5, alpha=5.0) with rank-based and level-based growth features for regime-invariant valuation prediction. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) growth percentile rank [0,1] — regime-invariant ordinal growth premium (rank is stable across bull/bear markets since it always maps to [0,1] regardless of the absolute growth distribution), (2) winsorized EPS growth% — cardinal magnitude preserving the actual growth rate information, (3) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (4) growth²/100 — convexity capturing exponential PE premium for high-growth firms. For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth rank, growth level, gross margin, and growth×margin interaction (growth more valuable for high-margin businesses). For EV/GP: 4 features using growth rank, growth level, positive indicator, and growth². PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 120) range with 3/97 winsorization. Huber loss (epsilon=1.5) naturally downweights outlier observations without removing them, providing more stable coefficient estimates than OLS across time periods. L2 regularization (alpha=5.0) for temporal stability. Rank features provide temporal stability since cross-sectional ordering is more persistent than absolute growth levels across market regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Huber robust regression (epsilon=1.35, alpha=5.0) with growth transforms for cross-sectional valuation prediction. Huber loss naturally downweights outlier observations (PE ratios have heavy tails from distressed/speculative names) without removing them, providing more stable coefficient estimates than OLS across time periods. For P/EPS: 4 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium on earnings, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing vs declining earnings (market pays substantial premium simply for positive earnings trajectory), (3) growth²/100 — convexity capturing exponential PE premium for high-growth firms (well-documented non-linearity in growth-PE relationship where marginal PE premium per unit of growth increases at higher growth rates), (4) growth × positive indicator — allows asymmetric slope where positive growth is rewarded more steeply than negative growth is punished (call-option-like payoff structure of equity means upside growth is more valuable than downside is destructive). For EV/Rev: 4 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin interaction, and growth² convexity (growth more valuable for high-margin businesses since each revenue dollar converts to more profit). For EV/GP: 4 features using growth, positive indicator, growth², and signed sqrt growth (concave transform capturing diminishing marginal valuation impact of extreme growth). PE observations pre-filtered to (2, 150) range. Target winsorized at 3/97 pctile for PE; growth at 3/97. L2 regularization (alpha=5.0) for temporal stability across market regimes. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.

Ridge regression (lambda=10) with growth features + FMP cross-sectional factors. For P/EPS: 8 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing firms, (3) growth²/100 — convexity in PE-growth relationship, (4) log_market_cap — size effect (larger firms trade at lower PE due to maturity/lower growth expectations), (5) earnings_yield — mean-reversion anchor (low earnings yield = expensive = high PE, captures valuation level persistence), (6) roic — quality premium (high ROIC firms deserve higher multiples due to superior capital allocation), (7) momentum_6m — price momentum drives multiple expansion/compression (winner stocks re-rate upward), (8) beta — risk discount (higher beta = higher discount rate = lower PE, captures systematic risk pricing). For EV/Rev: 7 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin, log_market_cap, rd_to_revenue (R&D-intensive firms trade at higher revenue multiples due to future growth optionality), roic, momentum_6m. For EV/GP: 6 features using growth, positive indicator, growth², log_market_cap, roic, momentum_6m. FMP factors are z-scored cross-sectionally with NaN mean-imputed to 0. PE observations pre-filtered to (0, 150). Target winsorized at 3/97 for PE; 2/98 for EV multiples. Growth winsorized at 3/97. FMP factors winsorized at 2/98. Ridge lambda=10 for temporal stability. All features have clear economic rationale.

Ridge regression (lambda=10) with growth features + FMP cross-sectional factors. For P/EPS: 6 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing firms, (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude effect, (4) log_market_cap — size effect (larger firms trade at lower PE due to maturity/lower growth expectations), (5) roic — quality premium (high ROIC firms deserve higher multiples due to superior capital allocation), (6) momentum_6m — price momentum drives multiple expansion/compression (winner stocks re-rate upward). For EV/Rev: 6 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin, log_market_cap, roic, momentum_6m. Ridge regression (lambda=10) on LOG-TRANSFORMED valuation multiples with growth features + FMP factors. Key innovation: regressing log(multiple) instead of raw multiple. Valuation multiples are right-skewed and the growth-multiple relationship is approximately multiplicative (not additive), so log-space regression naturally handles heteroscedasticity, reduces outlier influence, and stabilizes coefficients. This is consistent with standard finance theory where PE ≈ 1/(r-g) implies log(PE) is approximately linear in growth for moderate growth rates. For P/EPS: 6 features: (1) winsorized EPS growth% — linear growth premium, (2) positive growth indicator — discrete PE premium for growing firms, (3) absolute growth magnitude — captures symmetric magnitude/uncertainty effect, (4) log_market_cap — size effect (larger firms trade at lower PE), (5) roic — quality premium (high ROIC firms deserve higher multiples), (6) momentum_6m — price momentum drives multiple expansion/compression. For EV/Rev: 6 features using growth, gross margin, growth×margin, plus same 3 FMP factors. For EV/GP: 6 features using growth, positive indicator, log growth, plus same 3 FMP factors. FMP factors are z-scored cross-sectionally with NaN mean-imputed to 0 and winsorized at 1/99. Log target winsorized at 2/98. Growth winsorized at 3/97. Predictions exponentiated back to level space for evaluation. All features have clear economic rationale for cross-sectional valuation.
